speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about you were leaving--

speaker: interviewee
text: Leaving Druzkopol. We came to Sienkiewiczowka Ghetto. It was easier for us, because the Czechs-- the Czechs from the farm village used to bring us food. Throw it. You know, it was not allowed. For a, for a sin like this, they could have been killed, if the Ukrainian or German would see them. But they still did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was easier for us. We had little bits of food. And we had to go to work-- to all kind of, you know, dirty works, my father. So that was not a ghetto? Or that was a--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was also-- they also collected Jews from all the farms and villages and put it-- all together, there was 500 Jews. It's small. And there also were a few streets that the Jews lived, and the others where non-Jews lived. And we moved-- we lived with-- we-- my uncle took us into his apartment. He had a house. It was about 1, 2, 3, 4 rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which uncle was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's brother-- the one that sent us the papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yosel Krystol. And we lived-- my-- I, my brothers, and my father, we had one room. And my uncle had a room. And there was another family lived in my-- grandmother, my father. My grandfather died just four weeks after my mother died. And-- but my grandmother was half blind and lived with us. And it was hard. Very hard, I would say so. And we also-- the youth wanted to run away, because we already heard that there was a, there was a killing in that city and that city, that the Germans killed the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: But again, the older people didn't let us go. They were saying, if, if you run away, all the young people, who is going to be working? The old people work. But-- and they will kill us right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every, every night we used to get, like, to get a little bit-- it wasn't allowed, to go out after dark. We not-- we were not allowed to go outside. Had to be inside. And god forbid, if, if a little light would show on the outside that we're inside. Was a cardinal sin. Well, it was war. They were already fighting at that time. And that, so they, you know, were protecting themselves. And they didn't care about the Jews anyhow. So we did not run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you yourself want to run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we wanted--

speaker: interviewer
text: You.

speaker: interviewee
text: We wanted. Me and  my brother, my older brother, sure. And there were younger people that we wanted to run away, because we already heard that there are, in the woods, there are Partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't. We didn't yet do it, because one early morning that was 1942 and the second day of [NON-ENGLISH]. So it's like in October. Early in the morning, we heard knocking on the windows. And then we heard my mother's sister's  voice. She said children, [NON-ENGLISH]. Well, help yourself. The Germans are surrounded. The Germans, the Ukrainians, surrounded. And they taking all the Jews on last-- on big orders, and most probably to kill.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father, we all got up. And my father said to us three children, try to escape. And when you escape, try to go to the village, to the farm village. And there was a man by name Knierzaczek-- that family, Knierzaczek. They were very good friends of my parents. And [INAUDIBLE] will meet there-- whoever will survive will meet there. Gentiles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gentiles, yeah, Czechs. And as we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before, before we continue this, you were telling me before that you knew that there were Partisans in the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- you know, we didn't know that there are. But people were saying, talking about Partisans. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish Partisans? Jewish Partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Russian Partisans. And might be Jewish, some of the Jewish, but very little, because there was no time. It was-- everything [INAUDIBLE]. October of '42, it wasn't yet a year that they were here. And the Germans, they already were killing the-- I mean, liquidating the ghettos. You see, and not like in Poland. They took from the ghettos to concentration camps. Here they didn't. They killed right away the Jews. So whoever escaped just from the killing, you know, ran away to the woods or whatever, had to-- was lucky to survive. But very few, very few people really survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we went out-- when we ran out of the house, my poor grandmother, [NON-ENGLISH], put her in the basement. She was blind. She couldn't go no place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I heard my aunt says to the uncle, you go. I'll be with the children. You go. You try to save his life. But he never went. He stayed with her. We ran out-- my father, myself, and my two brothers. And as we walked out of the house, we saw the Germans, there are Germans. And Ukrainians most [INAUDIBLE] with their arms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Guns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with the guns. And if you try-- like, we were trying to go through them, because there was no-- you know, just to run out. So they killed right away. I saw quite a few people that fell right away. And then we split up. I don't know where anybody is. But there was a house. It was a house owned by my, by my friend's parents. Anyhow, I walked in in the back. There was like a barn. I walked in in this barn. And there was a step ladder to go up in the attics. So I went up to the attics.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I looked. The attic is full of Jews that I knew, people that I knew. And I didn't have where to hide. Everybody's hiding. How much is the attic? How much can you hide? So I just sat down by the step, you know, by the all where the step ladder was. And, and I hear all the way they are taking the Jews out of the houses and putting them in the autos. The screams. To this day, when I think about it, I hear the screams of the children and everything. And-- So in leaving, and running out, you got separated from everybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we got separated. They told me that my parents, my brothers were killed just not far where I was. I don't know if it's true. But they were killed. They never survived from it. And as I was sitting, I didn't have where to hide. I just heard all the screams. Then we heard Ukrainian-- I mean, the police, police people come up to the attic. And one of-- the first one that came up, there was like a schmata laying. He just threw it. And that schmata covered me, believe it or not. And I hear the way they throwing down all Jews. And I'm waiting only to be, you know, kicked, I should go. Nobody touched me. And I remained there. And I heard all the noises and everything. And then after a while, it got quiet. Then I heard the Ukrainian people going in in the Jewish houses and what was left there, there's nothing. The Germans already had taken everything anyhow. But whatever was left, they were taking it. Taking it and, you know, and I was sitting there. And it's got to get dark. And I didn't know what to do. I wanted to go down. There was no step ladder. So I jumped down. I don't know how I didn't kill myself. But I jumped down. And it was like on the outskirts, there was a railroad going through. And the railroad was a little bit high and here. So and I'm on my fours, I went out from this barn. And with the rails from the [INAUDIBLE], I was-- I tried to run away from there. And I did, I did. I don't know how long I went. But anyhow, I winded up in a, in a, what do you call it, woods. There were big woods in our area. And later on, I heard there was on a, on a crossing not far where I was, a policeman, a Ukrainian, and he saw me. But he didn't touch me. It was just plain luck. And when I came to the woods--

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you-- by the way, before we leave the topic of your brothers, how did you find out what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really didn't find out. But everybody knew that these people that didn't survive were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So nobody found their bodies or anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was-- they-- if they found it, they took them all on the outskirts of the city. There was already prepared big graves. And there was a-- you know, and all the Jews were killed and put in that grave. There was two big graves. I saw it once. And I fell very-- in the woods, I was exhausted. And I fell asleep. I slept I don't know how long. When I woke up, it was starting to get daylight.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I tried to figure out-- somehow, I figured it out. And I went to that farm village, to that Czech farmer that my father told us to go. And as I knock on his door, he comes out. And he, what do you call it? Makes the cross.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cross over himself. He said, what are you doing here? You can't stay here a minute, because they will looking for you right away here, because they know that I was a friend with your father. And he gave me a piece of bread and told me go away right away. Don't stay. So I had to leave. And there was, like, two villages on one side. And between the two villages was the public school and a cemetery. So I left him. And I went up to the public school, and because the teacher-- there was only one teacher at that school. I knew him, because I went to school before the war. He was my teacher. And before this, I had a friend, [? Anhamada, ?] worked in a place where they birth-- made birth certificates. Was probably like-- and she got-- she stole two blanks of birth certificates. And she gave me one, and to her sister-- to her daughter. And we were planning both of us to escape, because we knew-- we spoke very good Ukrainian, and Polish, and Czech. But it never materialized. But I had that piece of paper with me. But I couldn't-- I didn't have it what to fill it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a name on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Blank. So I went into this school. And the teacher was very nice to me. And I told him what I was afraid already to ask him if I could stay. I just asked him, could you help me to fill that out?

speaker: interviewer
text: So he did fill it out for me. I don't remember even the name. I never used it anyhow. And he gave me to eat. And he said, you know, the best thing is you don't try to stay here. Go to other villagers, to Czech farms, where they don't know you. Maybe you'll find a place where to stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told me how to get there, to those. So I went, but I wasn't successful for two, three days. All of them gave me food and felt sorry for me. But to let me stay by somebody, they didn't. And I was walking around maybe three days later. And I didn't have a place where to stay. So I decided I'm going back to Sienkiewiczowka. I just felt nobody is alive. Why should I-- I don't want to be alive either. And I was going back to Sienkiewiczowka. Meantime, a Czech family, farmers, were working. I remember the potatoes they were taking out out of the earth. And they saw me from afar. And she sent her son, which was a friend of mine. I went to school with him. And so  I was sure that he's going to tell-- take me straight to the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, no, my mother-- come, my mother wants to talk to you. So I went back with him to the field where his mother and father was there. And her mother was very nice. So she said, she didn't know me. But she just said, you know, I think your father is alive. And that gave me, like, a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she said-- she gave me something to eat, whatever she had. And she said, come. I'll take you to the woods. It was not far woods. And you'll sit there. There's another woman with three-- two-- three daughters from another town. And you'll see them. And in the evening, you'll come to my house. And you'll-- I'll give you what-- I didn't have. I left the house without shoes, just in a dress. And it was getting cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came at night to her house. And she gave me to eat and gave me warm clothes to wear, shoes and a coat, and told me, go back. And in the evening, you come back. Then, while I was sitting in the woods with these three-- the mother with the three daughters-- You didn't know them, did you, from before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't, because they weren't from our town, from someplace else, from [? Maropov. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, also escaped. And I heard people-- Czech-- a few women were going. And they were talking [INAUDIBLE]. You know, you know, Benjamin, is-- he escaped. He is alive someplace. And I heard it. And I just, you know, most probably so I find, like, an interest. So I didn't go back to Sienkiewiczowka. But I now I went back to my Czech woman. And the teacher's wife was a sister to this Czech. And somehow, I thought to myself, if I went, it was probably going to happen with my father too. He was probably went to that Knierzaczek, and he most probably didn't want to give him. So maybe he stopped by the teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told her. You know, go to your sister. Tell her that I'm alive. And I know my father is alive. Maybe she heard about it. Maybe she knows about him, something. And my father-- shall I tell you how my father escaped? The way I came up to that barn, not far was another barn with, with straw, where they keep for the-- my father ran into that place. And he hid himself between the straw. But then in the evening, there was a Czech owner. He came out. So my father wore a pair of good shoes and good pants. First of all, he took off the pants and the shoes. But he gave him another pair, another shoes. And he gave  him something to eat. He said, but please don't stay here. Go. So my father left. And it was the same story. He also went like I went. I didn't-- it was probably after me or before me. I really don't know. And then he went to the school, to the teacher. And he-- but he, my father, asked the teacher, can you hide me on, on some place? And the teacher did. So to this day, I don't remember if, if when I was-- if the-- if my father was already there. Maybe not. Maybe he came, like, a later. Anyhow, so this-- Didn't the teacher know that that was your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah sure, he knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if he would have been hiding there, wouldn't  he have told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't tell me anything. And I don't know who told his father-- my father later that he saw me. I don't remember it. But when I told to this Czech woman, go to your sister and tell her, if I'm not going to find my father, I'm going back to the city, to Sienkiewiczowka. So my-- she went. But two sisters, they were afraid to say to each other that they, that they, that they know that I'm with her, and that this one is with-- and that my father's back. They were afraid, because this was a terrible-- they could have got killed, the families, you know, for saving a Jew. But anyhow, it went out for a few days. And then she said once to her sister-- the teacher's wife said to her, you know, I heard that that's true. He's alive. Maybe he'll pass by. I'll tell him. She didn't tell him that that's [INAUDIBLE]. And then the truth is she told him. And that's how we met. My father came to the other Czech at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: That must-- not the forest. I was by this Czech in the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about the reunion when you first saw him.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very hard to describe. [CRYING] [INAUDIBLE]. But it was a savior for me. I would have never survived on my own. My father knew everybody. And they all liked him. And they all helped us. Like we-- that, that Czech woman that saved-- that let me go. We stayed by her about 10 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Chmuchalek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chmuchalek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chmuchalek. It's very hard names. I have it written down. The name is Chmuchalek. Anyhow, we stayed there [INAUDIBLE] we had to go to-- my father had to go and look for another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you and your father-- at that point when you found each other was when, about? What town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you when. It was about three weeks later, not a month even.

speaker: interviewer
text: So December '42.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was in October of '42. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you first saw each other, after you saw each other again, did you both know that your brothers were dead?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. In fact, when the Polish woman-- the Czech woman told me that when they, when the, when the teacher said to my father that I am alive, so he unintentionally said, not my son? Because my younger brother was a genius. But-- not that he didn't want me to be alive. But the first reaction, not my son? Because he was such a smart man and everything. Well, anyhow, for me, it was easier, in a way, because I had somebody to look, look out after me. My father used to go and find another place. We didn't stay in the houses. We stayed in the, in the barns and the-- what do you call the place-- the barn where they keep food for the, for the, for the horses and for the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Silo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that. So we used to make a hole-- my father used to make a hole in the, in the, in the hay or in the straw. And we used to hid there and lay there all day. And at night, whatever we had to do, we had to wait until at night . And my father used to go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this went on for how--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in hiding 19 months. That was-- the first winter in '42, it was a terrible, hard winter. And it was in November. The first snow fell out. We were by somebody in the barn. And somebody told the German, the Ukrainian, that there were Jews in that villages. And one morning, they surrounded the whole village. And they were looking for Jews. So naturally, we had-- where we were, we had to go away. So we went away to somebody else. Now, at night, and we hid again in the straw. And they didn't know that we are there. We laid there for about three, four-- I think it's for about four days and three nights. It's-- we didn't have no-- I mean, we had a little food, so it was finished. But the worst thing was the water, terrible. The tap water was off. Finally, my father said it's going to go out. And while-- and they did surrounded that, that Czech village. And they did find-- in fact, they found a couple that was my late husband's sister, and her husband, and a child. They must have been hiding by somebody. We don't know where. And when this-- when they-- they surrounded. So they told them to go away. So they went away in the fields. And it was the first snow. So by the steps, they found them.

speaker: interviewer
text: The tracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on a field. Ironically, that field belonged to a Jew. And that's where they were killed-- the three people, four people. There was another woman with them. They were killed then and buried there. And after the few days, it was just unbearable. So my father went out and got some water and some food from somebody. But he didn't tell them where he is. And this was going on for a few weeks that we were staying and nobody knew where we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would just go from barn to barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: To barn, yeah. And then on the top of it, I was getting pains in my stomach-- attacks. That was gallbladder attack. At that time, I didn't know what it is-- what it was. And no medicine, no nothing. My father used to go ask for food and drink, used to get a hot, hot water bottles. And that used to help me a little bit. Beside all the [NON-ENGLISH], so this was on the top of it. Then one evening, my father goes-- and it was Christmas night-- goes into a Czech family. When they saw him, they all made crosses. And she said she heard that I'm-- that your daughter is also alive. He said, yes. Please bring her in here. You'll stay with us for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Ribak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ribak.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And my father went and got me. And we came in. And that was a feast. She made us hot beds. We were full of lice. Unbelievable how people-- how we survived. Full of lice, bitten up, and she made us, made us hot beds. And you know, for that, if somebody would tell, they would be shot. Unbelievable what these nice Czech people did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why, though? Why do you think--

speaker: interviewee
text: They are good people-- just plain to-- a mitzvah. They believed in it. They are religious. They Catholics. They religious. And they believed saving a Jew is a mitzvah. And then we stayed overnight. And early in the morning, she took us to the barn. And she used to bring us, three times a day, food to eat. And her daughter, which went to school with me, was like friend. Used to come in, bring books, and used to sit with me and read. You know, it was unbelievable, in a time like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- how did you keep your spirits up, you and your father? How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know what to tell you. It's just--- it's-- you have such a will to live. I don't think it ever occurred to me that I'm going to be shot or killed. No, I just wanted to live. I wanted to live so badly. And so did my father-- very. And then that was going through the winter, like from one barn to the other. But when spring came, that was the hardest time, because there was no, no, no hay, no straw. We didn't have where to hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were just sitting in the attics on a barn. As we were sitting by one family-- what's the name was? I forgot the name. Oh, Hacken. No, that wasn't Hacken. He was the other one. I don't remember the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, we were sitting by this family. And it was-- we were all ready [INAUDIBLE]. Meantime, my late husband, who was Morris Resnick, he was hiding in another town, another village, just over-- it's like, when one village, and then the school, and then was another village.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's who-- his name was again? Morris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Morris Resnick. Yeah. And he was hiding with his brother in the place. And he used to go every middle of the night, go out and look for Jews, because you heard some people-- there is Jews. And where he stayed-- that was an aunt to the woman that where we stayed. And she told her aunt that we are with her. Her aunt didn't tell her. The other one didn't tell anybody that she  has anybody. In the middle of the night, my late husband came. The first time after what must have been about almost a year that we didn't see a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from the city, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know him?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the city, from the Sienkiewiczowka, from the ghetto. I knew him from the ghetto, actually. In fact, he had a brother, an older brother, in another town, [INAUDIBLE], and his daughter used to come for vacation to, to-- my late husband was married. He had a wife and a baby for a few months. And she used to come. And this girl was a friend of mine. His niece was my friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at the time that you saw your husband for the first time during the war, he was already-- he was married to somebody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was already dead. She was killed also in the ghetto with a baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he just appeared on your door one day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew through this woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: That he was coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: That he-- that aunt told him. How was it? Yeah, she told him most probably that we are down there. So he came to see us. He knew exactly--

speaker: interviewer
text: He stayed for a night?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just a few hours-- an hour or two. And we went back to his hiding place. And from then on, we were, like, in touch a little bit. That was the first Jew we met since the ghetto. And my husband, my late husband survived. He was-- he took office [INAUDIBLE] went to a Ukrainian village to look for a place to pay for his wife and child. And as he was coming back from this village, the ghetto was surrounded. So he never went in. So he went away. He was hiding. Happened that he came to this Czech's. And he had the best hiding place. Anyhow--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were, during this time period, 1942--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was '43 already, going to the '43. '44 we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in hiding in these horrible circumstances, how would you pass the day? How would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just not doing anything. Well, I used to get books. Like, when I was by this-- I was reading. And there were certain places that they let me go on down and help them with their laundry. And I was knitting a lot. And I knit sweaters for them. Things like that. In the cold weather, I used to sit and knit. Or sometimes they let me go in in the, in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever, at this point, encounter any Partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to get to it. Now, we were in one place. And it was also Hacken, yeah. And my father and I-- my father was in the barn. And they asked me to come down. They had two daughters. And we were washing clothes and, and having a good time, believe it or not, you know, talking. Meantime, the Ukrainian Banderovcis-- that's the Ukrainian Partisans-- they were horrible. They were the worst for Jews. They came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and they came into the house. And the woman and the two daughters didn't lose themselves. They served them food. And I helped them. And I don't know how I went through it. But they took, took me as one of, of the girls. They didn't know I'm Jewish. It took a few hours, and they left. They left. My father was up in hiding there in the straw. And I was-- and he didn't know what's happening to me. Anyhow, I survived that too. Then, while we were at that last place where my husband met us, it was-- we had to find a new place. My father went to look for a place. And I'm sitting. There is no place to hide. Only on the attic I'm sitting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meantime, I hear the Ukrainian Banderovcis are coming in. And a lot downstairs-- and I hear them talking. And I hear one of them is trying to go up to the barn, to the attics. And she says to, where are you going? We don't have no [INAUDIBLE]. Don't go.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I heard that, the roof was from straw. I don't know where I got the [NON-ENGLISH], the strength. And I pushed up straws. And I jumped down from the roof. And it was, like, in the back fields. [INAUDIBLE] I went back where the school, and that was-- and the cemetery. And I laid on that cemetery for hours. Then, then I heard it was quiet. So I-- Moshe told me, my late husband told me, where he's in hiding, by whom.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, we have to take-- we're going to take a break. And we'll pick up on that story, the end of that story.
